coredata.dc:title: Red-emissive carbon dots from spinach: Characterization and application in visual detection of time
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xiaokai
Cai, Lifeng
Hu, Guangqi
Mo, Luoqi
Zheng, Yihao
Hu, Chaofan
Lei, Bingfu
Zhang, Xuejie
Liu, Yingliang
Zhuang, Jianle
coredata.dc:description: As a new type of carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more and more attention due to their excellent properties. Here, we developed a simple, green and environmentally friendly method for preparing red-emissive CDs from spinach. The emission color of the CDs under UV light changed from red luminescence to green luminescence after one to four weeks. This was due to the change of the aggregated molecules on the surface of CDs. It was demonstrated that the nitrogen content of the CDs gradually decreased with the increase of time, since CDs combined with oxygen to form aggregates to eliminate excess nitrogen. It was further found that CDs showed the increase intensity of the emission in 500 nm wavelength and decrease intensity of the emission in 665 nm wavelength, which could be attributed to their respective emission from different molecular structures. Compared with the CDs stored for one week, the agglomeration of the CDs particles after four weeks was obvious, indicating that its light stability depended on the agglomeration structure of CDs. Due to their special properties, the CDs could be used for visual detection of time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
PEI
Time detection
Red emission
Color change

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images (a and c), particle size distribution (b), XRD pattern (d), FTIR spectrum (e), XPS survey spectrum (f) and high resolution scan of C 1s (g), N 1s (h) and O 1s (i) of CDs (dissolved in ethanol), respectively.

Emission spectra of CDs (dissolved in ethanol), excited at different wavelengths (a) and 400 nm wavelength (b). Inset: photographs of CDs (dissolved in ethanol) taken under 365 nm irradiation. c) Excitation spectrum of CDs (dissolved in ethanol), monitored at 665 nm. d) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs (dissolved in ethanol). e) Decay curves and lifetimes of CDs (dissolved in ethanol) monitored at 400 nm excitation.
a-c) TEM images of 2w-CDs, 3w-CDs and 4w-CDs (dissolved in ethanol), respectively. d-f) Particle size distribution of 2w-CDs, 3w-CDs and 4w-CDs (dissolved in ethanol), respectively.

a) The emission spectra of the different time-CDs (dissolved in ethanol) with excitation at 400 nm. b) Partial enlargement of a). c) Fluorescence decay curve of the different time-CDs (dissolved in ethanol) with excitation at 400 nm. d) CIE chromaticity diagram for the different CDs (dissolved in ethanol) with the photo of the different CDs (dissolved in ethanol) excited at 365 nm inset.
a) luminescence change of CDs solution with time. b) Color change after the CDs solution is applied to the filter paper with excitation at 365 nm.
Diagram for the synthesis process, structure and luminescent colors of CDs.

head.ce:title: Red-emissive carbon dots from spinach: Characterization and application in visual detection of time

Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new type of carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more and more attention due to their excellent properties. Here, we developed a simple, green and environmentally friendly method for preparing red-emissive CDs from spinach. The emission color of the CDs under UV light changed from red luminescence to green luminescence after one to four weeks. This was due to the change of the aggregated molecules on the surface of CDs. It was demonstrated that the nitrogen content of the CDs gradually decreased with the increase of time, since CDs combined with oxygen to form aggregates to eliminate excess nitrogen. It was further found that CDs showed the increase intensity of the emission in 500 nm wavelength and decrease intensity of the emission in 665 nm wavelength, which could be attributed to their respective emission from different molecular structures. Compared with the CDs stored for one week, the agglomeration of the CDs particles after four weeks was obvious, indicating that its light stability depended on the agglomeration structure of CDs. Due to their special properties, the CDs could be used for visual detection of time.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon-structured nanomaterials discovered in 2004. They have attracted great attention due to their unique optical and low toxicity properties [1–5]. CDs are widely used in different fields, such as environment, biology and life sciences [6–8]. There have been many studies on the use of convenient hydrothermal methods to synthesize CDs. The most important disadvantages are the consumption of expensive chemical products and the use of organic solvents (such as citric acid [9,10], glucose [11], urea [10] and thiourea [9]). These shortcomings limit their application in the environment, biology and life sciences. In fact, compared with man-made carbon sources, environmentally friendly natural products have many advantages in the preparation of CDs, including low prices and high abundance [12,13]. Therefore, it is necessary to explore an easily available and cheap carbon source to synthesize high-quality CDs with excellent photoluminescence (PL) behavior through environmentally friendly green technologies. For instance, CDs fabricated from different carbon sources like gas soot [14] and milk [15]. In addition, plant raw materials have proven to be an excellent source of carbon nanomaterial synthesis because of their high carbon content (e.g., grass [16], aloe [17], rose flowers [18], rice husks [19], coriander leaf [20], leek [21]). However, these tunable emissive spectra of CDs are mostly limited in-between the blue and green regions. Recently, advances to explore the nontoxic-biocompatible probes working in the long wavelength region of the spectrum (red and near-infrared (NIR)) for the in vivo photoluminescent imaging have gained a lot of interest [22–24]. Therefore, the synthesis of red-emissive CDs from natural biomass, especially plant leaves, is of great significance.

CDs have been used in detection in many ways due to their unique properties like excitation-dependent emission, low cytotoxicity and good aqueous solubility [25–27]. On the one hand, changes in their fluorescence properties occur through different mechanisms, such as resonance energy transfer, internal filtering effects, light-induced electron and charge transfer. CDs can be used to determine the cellular level of biomolecules and ions (bio-sensor), such as NO2[28], H2S [29], ascorbic acid [30], DPA [31], glucose [32], etc. On the other hand, the interaction of metal ions with the surface functional groups of CD leads to the formation of new electron-hole recombination with the help of energy transfer pathways and changes in the fluorescence properties of CDs. CDs could also be applied for the determination of ions (chemical-sensing), such as Cu2+ [33], Hg2+ [34−36], Co2+ [37], Pb2+ [38], etc. However, CDs in the field of time detection have not been found to be stimulated by ultraviolet light based on filter paper. Based on the sensitivity of CDs with time, it is necessary to realize the detection of time by CDs.

This work is based on the extraction of spinach to obtain carbon nanomaterials, which are modified by PEI molecules to obtain red-emissive carbon dots. As the oxygen molecules break the dispersed state of the CDs, the agglomeration of the CDs causes the luminescence to gradually change from red to green. Paper sensors have been widely used in the analysis of blood, cell, microRNA, and environmental pollutants in recent years due to their advantages of low cost, portability, and versatility [39,40]. Here, the paper sensor can be made by adding CDs to the filter paper. The sensor shows periodic change from red to green under UV lamp in one month to achieve the purpose of time detection. More importantly, the red-emissive CDs are rich in sources and suitable for large-scale production applications.

Polyethylenimine (PEI, Mw = 25,000) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Fresh Spinach was obtained from local markets. Ethanol and acetone were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout this work.

Spinach leaves (500 g) were washed carefully and cut into small pieces of about 1 cm square. The pieces were then immersed in a mixed solution of ethanol and acetone for 24 h(volume ratio of 1:1). The suspension was filtered through a filter paper and the extraction was collected. After rotary evaporating at 50 °C, 100 mL of thick chlorophyll solution were obtained. Finally, the chlorophyll solution was freeze-dried to obtain a chlorophyll powder.

First, chlorophyll powder (0.1 g) was dissolved in a PEI aqueous solution (10 mL PEI and 50 mL H2O) and then hydrothermally treated at 140 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μM microporous membrane and then dialyzed through a 3500 molecular weight dialysis bag for one day. Finally, a purified chlorophyll CDs solution was obtained.
2w-CDs, 3w-CDs and 4w-CDs refer to the carbon dots of the carbon dot solution after being dissolved in water for two weeks, three weeks, and four weeks, respectively.

First, the filter paper was cut into a 5cm * 20cm rectangular shape. The carbon dots just prepared are then dripped onto the filter paper strip after cooling. Finally, the prepared carbon dot label paper is placed at room temperature. Within one month, the emission color of the filter paper under UV lamp is gradually changed from red to green to achieve a visual time detection effect.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi. FT-IR spectra were measured on a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were taken on F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples in aqueous solution were carried out using a Shimadzu UV-2550 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded with a FEI Tecnai 12 transmission electron microscope and the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were recorded using a JEOL-2010 transmission electron microscope. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were measured using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800). The XRD patterns were performed using powder X-ray diffraction (Rigaku-Ultima IV, Japan) with Cu Kα (Kα1 = 0.1541 nm) irradiation at 36 kV and 30 mA with a scanning rate of 8° min −1. Decay curves and lifetimes were measured by Fluorescence Life Tester (Quantaurus Tau C11367).

The synthesis process of CDs mainly includes the extraction of spinach leaves and hydrothermal treatment. The chlorophyll molecule is hydrothermally carbonized to form CDs. (Figs. 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 See Scheme 1 ). The PEI chain in water is attached to the CDs through the strong attraction between the positive charge of PEI and the negative charge of CDs [41,42]. As a nitrogen-containing polymer, PEI has great potential to modify CD characteristics by modifying the CD surface. Most importantly, the PEI complex is an oxygen carrier, which is soluble in water and selectively reacts with oxygen molecules in the water [43]. Therefore, CDs can react with oxygen molecules in water and change the bonding mode between CDs. Interestingly, the synthesized CDs changed from a red light to a green light within a month. In order to further explore the role of the PEI solvent, water and PEI solvent are used to respectively generate CDs by hydrothermal reaction. As shown in Figure S1, the CDs formed in water are dark blue, while the CDs generated in PEI are red with excitation at 400 nm. The results show that PEI molecules play an important role in the formation of CDs. However, CDs can be stably present in the ethanol molecule so that the luminescent color does not change (Figure S2). Generally, CDs have a suitable band gap for visible light emissions due to their structural defects, elementary composition, and surface groups [44]. Particle size, surface charge, element content and type of functional group are the key factors affecting the luminescence mechanism of CDs [45–47]. Therefore, the relationship between the particle size, surface charge, element content, and type of functional group for CDs and the luminescent color at different times is explored below.

The as-prepared CDs appear as uniform microspherical particles with average diameter of about 3 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 a and b. From the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1c), it is apparent that CDs display well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm and 0.15 nm, which is close to the (100) and (220) diffraction facets of graphite carbon. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. 1d show a broad prominent peak at about 23.04°, which is similar to the interlayer spacing of graphite [48]. The chemical compositions and bands of CDs are further characterized. As shown in the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. 1e), the several stretching vibrations associated with bonded hydrogen as O–H at 3378 cm−1, C–H at 2947 cm−1 and 2837 cm−1. The peaks at 1572 cm−1 and 1471 cm−1 were merged arise due to aryl C–N stretching vibrations, confirming the embedding of nitrogen. The peaks at 1313 cm−1 were merged arise due to unsymmetrical stretching vibrations of –CH2 group. The peak at 1111 cm−1 and 1057 cm−1 were ascribed to the -C-O- stretching vibrations. More insights into the surface functional groups of CDs can be obtained from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. 1f). The XPS spectra showed the CDs are composed of C, N and O elements, corresponding to the peaks at 285, 398 and 531 eV. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) proves the existence of two peaks at 284.96 and 287.27 eV are ascribed to C–C/CC and CO/CN, respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1h) exhibits two peaks that are assigned to amine N and graphitic N, which are assigned to pyrrolic C2–N–H and graphitic N–C3. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1i) involves two peaks that are attributed to CO, C–OH/C–O–C, indicating the existence of oxygen-rich groups [49]. As mentioned above, the CDs are composed of large π-conjugated domains and shows the formation of polyaromatic structures.

As revealed in Fig. 2 a and b, CDs displays an emission center at 665 nm. The emission band at 665 nm wavelength originates from the functionalization of porphyrin [50]. The excitation spectra of CDs monitored at 665 nm are shown in Fig. 2c. The excitation band in the 400 nm and 500 nm can be assigned to the transitions from π states to the CO and CN surface states on the surfaces of CDs. CDs have sharp excitation peaks near the position of red emission, suggesting that electron transitions can occur from ground states to π* states. For further understanding the variation of CDs, the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 2d. The absorption band peaked at 400 nm and 656 nm are typically ascribed to the Soret band and the Q-band features of porphyrins [51,52]. As shown in Fig. 2e, the lifetime of photo-excitation for CDs were calculated to be 5.18 ns, which suggests that charge transfer happens so quickly that local state energy levels in CDs are always filled with electrons.

In order to demonstrate the relationship between luminescent properties and structure of CDs, the TEM images of the different time-CDs are taken in Fig. 3 a–c. Fig. 3d–f shows the particle size distribution of the CDs during the second, third and fourth weeks. Fig. 3a is TEM image of the CDs at two weeks, and it can be found that the CDs are uniformly distributed with good dispersion. From Fig. 3d, it can be found that the 2w-CDs is about 3–5 nm in the second week. Fig. 3b shows the TEM image of the corresponding CDs, which can be seen that the CDs agglomeration phenomenon is obvious. It is shown from Fig. 3e that the CDs have a particle size distribution of about 5–7 nm at three weeks. As shown in Fig. 3f, it can be found from the particle size distribution that the particle size of the CDs is approximately 7 nm. The DLS method is used to test the hydrated particle size of 2w-CDs, 3w-CDs and 4w-CDs as shown in Table S1 and Figure S3. The mean particle size of 2w-CDs, 3w-CDs and 4w-CDs are 5.48 nm, 7.03 nm and 8.28 nm, respectively, which indicates the high dispersion of CDs and the structure of abundant functional groups on the surface. From the corresponding TEM image in Fig. 3c, it is found that the CDs show an agglomeration effect. The above phenomenon corresponds to the mechanism, as shown in equation (1); [42,43]:
(1)
PEI − CDs
+
O 2
→
N 5
− CD −
O 2
2 −
− CD −
N 5
+ 2 N

The mechanism is supposed to be that the CDs react with oxygen molecules in the water to form a polymer structure, and it is known that the particle size of the agglomerated state is about 7 nm in four weeks. Furthermore, the CDs showed obvious emission decay in 5 days after bubbling with O2 for 10 min while the CDs bubbled with N2 showed weak emission decrease (Figure S4), which proved that the increased oxygen concentration in the solvent accelerated the color change of CDs.

However, when the CDs are dissolved in ethanol, a series of reactions can occur so that the color does not change at any time. The hydrogen atom on the hydroxyl group in the ethanol molecule is connected to the oxygen atom with strong electronegativity. Therefore, when O ̄ is close to the hydrogen atom at the hydroxyl end, strong ion-induced dipole interaction allows the two molecules to rapidly recombine directly. The reaction channels of oxygen anion and ethanol are mainly (2)–(6)[53]:
(2)
O -
+
CH 3
CH 2
OH → OH
+
CH 3
CH 2
O -
(3)
O -
+
CH 3
CH 2
OH →
OH -
+
CH 3
CH 2
O
(4)
O -
+
CH 3
CH 2
OH →
OH -
+
CH 3
HCOH
(5)
O -
+
CH 3
CH 2
OH →
OH -
+
CH 2
CH 2
OH
(6)
O −
+
CH 3
CH 2
OH →
CH 2
= CHO
−
+
H
+
H 2
O

The reaction (2) is proton transfer channels on the hydroxyl group, the reaction (3) is a hydrogen atom transfer channel on the hydroxyl group, the reaction (4) is a α-H transfer channel, the reaction (5) is a β-H transfer channel, and the reaction 6 is an enol negative ion channel. Multiple reaction channels dissociate the polymer. To further explore the effects of ethanol, CDs are hydrothermally produced in ethanol (OH-CDs), as shown in Figure S5. The prepared OH-CDs showed red light emission under UV light excitation. The emission didn't change over time. The emission spectrum is shown in Figure S6, displaying 672 nm emission position at 400 nm excitation. The absorption spectrum of OH-CDs exhibit strong absorption peaks at 414 nm and 654 nm, which come from the Soret band and the Q-band features of porphyrins, similar to the absorption spectrum of CDs (Figure S7).

To further explore the 2w-CDs, 3w-CDs and 4w-CDs, their XPS results are shown in Figure S8a. XPS survey scan confirms the existence of C, N and O in the different CDs. The XPS survey scan showed the peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV and 531 eV associated to the presence of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. Moreover, 4w-CDs has the highest content of C element and the lowest content of N element. In order to explore the specific changes of the different binding sites of C, N and O, we tested the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s for 2w-CDs as shown in Figure S8b, which displays the several C-binding sites at 284.93 eV (C–C/CC), 287.46 eV(CO/CN), 291.79 eV (COOH) and 294.62 eV(pi-pi*). The deconvulation of N1s and O1s show peaks at 398.45 eV (amine N), 398.95 eV (graphitic N), 530.48 eV (CO) and 531.94 eV (C–OH/C–O–C) in Figure S8c and S8d. Similarly, the deconvulation of C1s, N1s and O1s for 3w-CDs and 4w-CDs are shown in Figure S8e-j. It can be concluded that the amount of nitrogen gradually decreased as the CDs was placed for longer periods of time because CDs were combined with oxygen to form agglomerates to eliminate excess nitrogen, as shown in equation (1). The gradual decrease in amine N assigned to pyrrolic C2–N–H is attributed to the plundering of the amine N by the oxygen molecule.

The emission spectra of the different CDs under 400 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 4 a and b. As can be seen, the CDs mainly have two luminescence centers located in 665 nm and 500 nm regions. The emission spectrum of CDs solution is compared with the PEI solution after hydrothermal treatment (Figure S9). It is found that the 500 nm emission comes from the PEI molecule. Therefore, it can be inferred that the 665 nm light is derived from the functionalization of porphyrins, and 500 nm light is originated from the PEI molecule. As time passed, the red light emission of the CDs gradually decreases while the green light portion gradually increases. In addition, the PL quantum yield of the 1w-CDs, 2w-CDs, 3w-CDs and 4w-CDs (Table S2) showed that the quantum yield of CDs became lower over time. As shown in Fig. 4c, the PL decay times of each sample were measured. Obviously, the average lifetime from 2w-CDs to 4w-CDs increased from 5.28 ns to 5.38 ns, monitoring the emission at 665 nm wavelength, indicating that aggregation has little effect on the fluorescence lifetime of these CDs. The above results demonstrate that CDs possess excellent and uniform optical properties, and their tunable PL emission can be attributed to the surface state controlled by oxygen molecules. The different luminescent CDs are shown in Fig. 4d. The luminescence of the CDs gradually changes from red light to green light in CIE chromaticity diagram, which visually shows the trend of the color of luminescent CDs.

Fig. 5 a shows the emission color of the CDs gradually changes from red light to green light with time. This change of emission color of CDs with time offers the development of their application in time detection. As a proof of concept, we designed the detection label by coating CDs solution on the filter paper. As shown in Fig. 5b, the filter paper changed gradually from red to green as the time passed by. This kind of label is very good for time detection. For example, fresh milk with a shelf life of one month can be used to screen out expired milk under the excitation of UV light.

In summary, under the simple hydrothermal reaction, CDs with red light emission can be efficiently synthesized from spinach. The results show that chlorophyll molecules can react with PEI molecules in water to form carbon dots with rich amino groups on the surface. The amino-rich CDs contact the oxygen molecules in the air to change the aggregation state of the carbon dots, thus showing different PL characteristics. More importantly, the CDs were added to the filter paper to make paper sensor, which changed periodically from red to green under UV light within one month to achieve the purpose of time detection. This work provides a new way for the green synthesis of red-emissive CDs, which may find practical application in the field of food safety.

Xiaokai Xu: Methodology, Visualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Lifeng Cai: Resources, Data curation. Guangqi Hu: Software, Investigation. Luoqi Mo: Visualization, Methodology. Yihao Zheng: Software, Formal analysis. Chaofan Hu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Bingfu Lei: Resources, Formal analysis. Xuejie Zhang: Validation, Formal analysis. Yingliang Liu: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision. Jianle Zhuang: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51602108), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2017A030313256, 2020A1515011210), and Open Project Funding of Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Ecology-Toxicological Effects& Control for Emerging Contaminants (PY18004).
